comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the historical context and usability of the Stdwin window interface developed by Guido van Rossum in the late 1980s. Commenters reflect on its resource efficiency, portability challenges, and the evolution of cross-platform desktop applications leading up to modern solutions like Electron.

comments.author: decasia
comments.text:
It's a neat project. Write cross platform desktop apps in C. Presumably it would not have been very usable in practice in the late 1980s, because of all the OTHER system interfaces that still weren't portable, even if the windowing system was available in a portable way.

I can remember the subsequent period in which Java desktop apps were relatively common. They had cross platform UI by default. But the problem was:

1) cross platform GUIs are ugly by default, compared to fully native desktop apps, because they don't entirely replicate the affordances or the style of the platform;

2) in the Java case, it seemed heavyweight to install and sluggish compared to native apps;

Point 2 would not have applied to stdwin, as it would have produced small compiled binaries I suppose, but Point 1 would have.

So in the end, obviously web apps (and partly, Flash) took over the niche that "cross platform desktop apps" had once tried to fill, and then it was something of a dead zone until Electron, as far as I remember.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced and historically informed perspective on the challenges of cross-platform GUI development, accurately noting the trade-offs and evolution of technologies.

comments.author: spacedcowboy
comments.text: I remember running this on an Atari ST with MiNT as the multi-tasking kernel. It took a lot less resources from my 4MB STe than running X11 did. It was actually quite usable to compile with gcc in one window and edit (with vi, of course) in the other.
comments.analysis: This firsthand account adds valuable practical insight into the resource efficiency and usability of Stdwin compared to contemporaneous systems like X11.

comments.author: DaOne256
comments.text:
This should be linked as the original post: https://ir.cwi.nl/pub/5998

It says 1988 there.
comments.analysis: Providing the original source link and date is helpful and accurate, supporting the historical context of the discussion.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
How old is this? 1989 or something like that? Guido was probably quite young when he wrote it. Looks like LaTeX?

Edit: Someone else wrote 1988 which I suppose makes sense, as the latest reference at the end is from 1988 too. So then Guido was 32 years old.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly approximates the date and provides some biographical context, though the age calculation is slightly off since Guido was born in 1956, making him around 32 in 1988.

comments.author: pantulis
comments.text: I find it amusing that the paper doesn't include any screenshots.  Those were the days!
comments.analysis: This is a lighthearted observation about the style of academic papers from that era; it adds flavor but no substantive information.

comments.author: ale42
comments.analysis: This single-year comment adds minimal value beyond confirming the date already mentioned.
